title: Why the Dinosaur Extinction Changed Everything

lesson.learn:
The end-Cretaceous extinction was one of the most important turning points in Earth history. A large asteroid struck near what is now Chicxulub, Mexico, sending dust, soot, and sulfur into the atmosphere and sharply reducing sunlight. Combined with ongoing volcanic activity and ecological stress, the impact triggered a global crisis that wiped out the non-avian dinosaurs and many other species.

Its significance goes beyond the dinosaurs themselves. Once the dominant large animals disappeared, mammals were able to diversify into ecological roles that had previously been closed off. In that sense, the extinction did not just end an era. It created the conditions for the rise of the world humans would eventually inherit.
lesson.simpler: An asteroid impact helped end the age of dinosaurs. When the dinosaurs disappeared, mammals had room to grow into many new kinds of animals.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The dinosaur extinction changed everything because it reset the planets ecosystems and opened the door for mammals to rise.
lesson.deeperDive:
The Chicxulub impact sent a pulse of energy through the atmosphere and likely triggered an impact winter, with sunlight blocked by dust and soot. That would have collapsed food chains on land and in the oceans. The fossil record also shows that the extinction was not just about one event. Deccan Traps volcanism, climate stress, and ecological disruption all contributed to a world that was already under pressure.

Not everything died. Birds survived, as did some small mammals, reptiles, and plants. After the extinction, mammals underwent adaptive radiation and eventually filled many of the ecological niches left open by the dinosaurs. The result was a radically changed planet. Without that reset, the evolutionary path that eventually led to humans would have looked very different.
lesson.funFact: Birds are the living descendants of dinosaurs, which means the dinosaur story never fully ended.
references: Deccan Traps and end-Cretaceous extinction studies
Paleontological research on mammal diversification after the K-Pg boundary

people: Luis Alvarez - physicist associated with the asteroid impact hypothesis
Walter Alvarez - geologist who helped develop the impact explanation
Jan Smit - geologist whose work supported the Chicxulub theory
Paleontologists - researchers who study the fossil evidence
places: Chicxulub, Mexico - site of the impact crater
Hell Creek Formation - famous fossil region in North America
The Deccan Traps - volcanic province linked to end-Cretaceous stress
Gubbio, Italy - place associated with the iridium layer evidence
years: 66 million years ago - the end-Cretaceous extinction
Present day - extinction research continues to refine the story
concepts: Mass extinction - a rapid loss of many species
Impact winter - global cooling caused by atmospheric debris
Iridium anomaly - evidence associated with the asteroid impact
Adaptive radiation - rapid diversification after extinction
Ecological reset - the rebuilding of ecosystems after collapse
